description: This neuron detects line breaks or newlines that separate distinct sections of text, particularly after titles, headers, or concluding statements.
This neuron detects the beginning of a word or token, particularly at the start of proper nouns, technical terms, or formatted text blocks.
This neuron detects the beginning of proper nouns, titles, or capitalized words that start new topics, entries, or sections in lists and documents.
This neuron detects newline characters that immediately follow capital letters, particularly at the beginning of titles, headings, or proper nouns.
Hyphens or dashes used as separators in HTML reference attributes and citation linking syntax.
This neuron detects words related to assumptions, estimations, or conditional statements in formal or technical contexts, particularly in insurance, legal, or business documentation.

This neuron detects hyphens used to connect or separate compound terms, particularly in technical contexts involving paired or contrasting concepts (like upper-lower, phase connections, or coupling parts).
The neuron appears to detect line breaks or section breaks that separate a headline or title from the beginning of the article body or subsequent content.
This neuron detects semicolons that terminate initialization statements immediately before for-loop declarations.
This neuron detects citations, references, or numerical identifiers in academic/technical text, particularly those appearing in reference lists or inline citations.
This neuron detects the word "applicable" in legal/license text contexts, particularly in Apache License boilerplate and formal documentation describing conditional situations.
This neuron detects the opening angle bracket or brace that begins a code block, markup tag, or data structure (specifically `<` in XML/HTML or `{` in JSON/PHP).
This neuron detects periods that end declarative sentences, particularly in formal or technical writing such as academic abstracts, product descriptions, and informational texts.
This neuron detects line breaks or newlines that precede horizontal rule separators (------) or the start of new comment sections in online forum discussions.

This neuron detects the prefix "pre" when it appears as part of compound words or hyphenated terms, particularly in technical, formal, or descriptive contexts (e.g., "precompiled," "preplanned," "pre-populated," "preeminent," "precision," "preclean," "prefecture").

This neuron detects the prefix "inter-" when it appears at the beginning of compound words (such as "interns," "international," "interments," "interoperable," "intermolecular," "interdigitation," "interposed").
This neuron detects the prefix "non" when it appears as part of a hyphenated compound word or technical term, particularly in scientific and academic contexts.

This neuron detects the prefix "hyper-" (or "hyp-") in medical and technical terms, particularly when it forms compound words indicating excess, overactivity, or abnormal increase in a biological or chemical condition.
This neuron detects closing HTML/XML tags, specifically the opening bracket of a closing tag (`</`) in template or markup code.
This neuron detects the word "Invention" when it appears in the standard "Field of the Invention" section header of patent documents.

This neuron detects hyphens that are part of compound phrases or terms, particularly those involving "All" in names of teams, organizations, or descriptive phrases (like "All-Century," "All-for-Ireland," "All-Time").
The neuron detects lines containing three or more consecutive hyphens, typically appearing at the start of YAML document separators, comment dividers, or file diffs.

This neuron detects the opening delimiter of template tags or code blocks, specifically the "{%" or "{" characters that begin template directives, variable substitutions, or code syntax in various templating languages and programming contexts.
